attributes.stem: A 9-month-old boy is brought by his mother to the Emergency Department with a 24-hour history of abdominal pain. He experiences episodes of pain that appear to last for several minutes, during which he cries and pulls up his knees. He has also vomited yellow material several times. His medical history is unremarkable and his mother received all the standard antenatal tests and imaging which were normal. He was born by uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery at 38 weeks, with a birth weight of 5 kg, and he passed meconium 26 hours after birth. Upon examination, the boy's abdomen is slightly distended and there is a palpable mass in the right upper quadrant.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following signs is most likely to be seen on imaging?
options: Corkscrew appearance on upper gastrointestinal series
Double bubble sign on abdominal X-ray
attributes.explanation:
This child is almost certainly experiencing episodes of abdominal colic and the vignette is classic of intussusception. Intussusception occurs when a proximal part of the bowel invaginates into a distal part. Prevalence usually peaks at 4-9 months and declines around 18 months. The cause is unknown for most cases, but enlargement and invagination of Peyer's patches following a viral infection or immunisation is a well-recognised factor. Some cases may be due to a Meckel's diverticulum, but all other conditions that might cause a lead point are rare. Most of the time, intussusception occurs in the ileocecal region, where the ileum folds into the caecum. The trapped section of the bowel is vulnerable to ischaemia due to compression of the blood supply; this may result in sloughing off mucosa, blood and mucous in the stool resulting in a 'red currant jelly' stool. However, this only occurs in the minority of cases. According to a literature review of acute intestinal intussusception, the cardinal symptoms are intermittent abdominal pain, non-bilious and bilious vomiting and red-currant jelly stool. This triad has a positive predictive value of 93% but only affects 7.3%-40% of patients. Additional symptoms include pulling legs to the chest and later signs include a sausage-shaped mass and presence of fever if there is bowel necrosis.

Target sign is the characteristic finding seen in intussusception on ultrasound. The target sign refers to the invaginated portion of the bowel appearing as rings on a "target" in a transverse section on ultrasonography. Ultrasound is the diagnostic test of choice with a sensitivity and negative predictive value close to 100% and specificity of 88-100%.

Corkscrew appearance on upper gastrointestinal series refers to the twisted configuration of proximal small bowel loops seen in malrotation with a midgut volvulus. Midgut volvulus occurs when the intestine twists upon itself due to intestinal malrotation occurring during embryological development. This condition is less likely to be the cause for this child's presentation as it most commonly occurs during the first few weeks of life and is characterised by initial sudden onset bilious vomiting, upper abdominal distension and tenderness, haemodynamic instability and haematochezia (if bowel ischaemia is present).

The double bubble sign on abdominal X-ray represents a gas-distended dilated stomach and proximal duodenum due to obstruction in the midpart of the duodenum. The presence of the double bubble with absence of distal bowel gas in a neonate represents complete obstruction and is diagnostic of duodenal atresia. However, the presence of some distal bowel gas is indicative of a partial obstruction which has a variety of causes - duodenal stenosis/web, annular pancreas, and midgut volvulus. This patient is unlikely to have duodenal atresia as it usually presents with bilious vomiting within the first 24-48 hours of life after the first feed and progressively worsens. They may also have a distended upper abdomen and delayed meconium passage. Furthermore, the mother received the required antenatal ultrasounds, which showed no abnormalities. Typically, duodenal atresia is characterised by the presence of a double bubble and polyhydramnios in antenatal scans. There is also no mention of chromosomal conditions like Down syndrome, which is associated with a significant portion of duodenal atresia cases.

Pneumatosis intestinalis is the presence of gas in the bowel and is relatively rare in children, but if found, is typically in premature infants with necrotising enterocolitis (NEC). It is characterised by collections of gas, usually in a linear fashion, parallel to the bowel wall producing a 'tram track' appearance. A few of the causes include those leading to mucosal disruption such as Hirschsprung disease, inflammatory bowel disease and neutropenic colitis. Necrotising enterocolitis is very unlikely in this patient as it most commonly occurs in the first 1-2 weeks of birth, and he also does not display risk factors such as low birth weight, prematurity or neonatal asphyxia. Presentation of NEC is highly variable, but is usually characterised by fatigue, bilious vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal tenderness, abdominal distension, absent bowel sounds and signs of haemodynamic compromise.

Acute appendicitis is characterised by a distended appendix on ultrasound. The most informative sign for this condition is an outer diameter of the appendix measuring 6 mm or more on cross-section. The typical presentation of acute appendicitis begins with periumbilical or generalised abdominal pain, which then localises to the right lower quadrant (RLQ) within the last 12-24 hours. Tenderness is present in the RLQ, along with symptoms such as nausea, anorexia, and a low-grade fever. It is unlikely to be appendicitis as the patient is not between the ages of 10-20 years (peak incidence of appendicitis). Palpable mass in the right side of the abdomen occurs in appendicitis with complication and features of systemic sepsis.
learningPoints: Target sign is the characteristic finding seen in intussusception on ultrasound representing the invaginated portion of the bowel.
A corkscrew appearance on upper gastrointestinal series refers to the twisted configuration of proximal small bowel loops seen in malrotation with a midgut volvulus.
references: Gluckman S, Karpelowsky J, Webster AC, et al. Management for intussusception in children (Review) Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2017 Jun 1;6(6):CD006476.
